_work.title: The Heidelberg Book of Fate (Cod. Pal. germ. 832)
_work.creator: Anonymous (workshop of Ludwig Henfflin?)
_work.rights: Public Domain Mark 1.0. Universitätsbibliothek Heidelberg, Cod. Pal. germ. 832.
_work.blurb: Folios from the illustrated *Heidelberger Schicksalsbuch* — a late-15th-century German Losbuch (book of fate), its lot-tables and coloured oracle-miniatures used to answer questions by the casting of dice. Sourced via IIIF from the open, public-domain digitisation at the University Library Heidelberg.

entries.title: Lot-table from the Heidelberg Book of Fate (fol. 11v)
entries.work: The Heidelberg Book of Fate (Cod. Pal. germ. 832)
entries.creator: Anonymous (German, workshop of Ludwig Henfflin?)
entries.summary:
A lot-table from the *Heidelberger Schicksalsbuch*, the apparatus by which the book of fate was consulted.

## Iconography
A grid of numbers ruled in red and black fills the page in columns. Each entry routes the querent — after casting dice on a question-page — onward through the book's branching structure toward a verse-answer. The whole page is a working divinatory algorithm rendered as a table.

## Significance
The medieval *Losbuch* (book of lots) was a sortilege machine: a reader posed one of a fixed set of questions, threw dice, and followed the resulting number through tables, pointer-figures, and 'judges' to a rhymed oracle. Such books sat at the licit edge of divination, framed as a game yet drawing on the same impulse as astrology and geomancy. This copy is one of the most lavishly illustrated to survive.

## For artists & game designers
A literal pre-modern randomisation table — the original 'roll on the fate chart' mechanic, beautifully laid out.

entries.title: Oracle miniatures from the Heidelberg Book of Fate (fol. 41v)
entries.work: The Heidelberg Book of Fate (Cod. Pal. germ. 832)
entries.creator: Anonymous (German, workshop of Ludwig Henfflin?)
entries.summary:
A page of four coloured oracle-miniatures from the *Heidelberger Schicksalsbuch*.

## Iconography
Four small framed paintings are set into the text in a two-by-two array, each captioned in red and black. The miniatures illustrate the oracle's verse-answers — figures, scenes, and emblems that the querent reaches at the end of a chain of dice-throws.

## Significance
The illustrations are not decoration but signposts in the divinatory machine: the *Losbuch* threads the reader through question-pages, number-tables, and pointer-figures to one of these painted answers. The genre fused entertainment, moral counsel, and genuine fortune-telling, and its picture-cycles are a rich, under-studied seam of late-medieval popular esoterica.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made 'oracle deck' page — discrete illustrated outcomes, each with its caption, exactly as a fate-table result screen.

entries.title: Oracle-figures from the Heidelberg Book of Fate (fol. 76v)
entries.work: The Heidelberg Book of Fate (Cod. Pal. germ. 832)
entries.creator: Anonymous (German, workshop of Ludwig Henfflin?)
entries.summary:
Four coloured oracle-figures with their verse-answers, from the *Heidelberger Schicksalsbuch*.

## Iconography
Four framed miniatures sit among the red-and-black text. In the *Losbuch* tradition such figures act as the 'masters', 'judges', or pointer-personifications who deliver the fate the dice have selected — each paired with a rhymed German prognostication.

## Significance
The *Schicksalsbuch* organises its divination around a cast of recurring figures (planets, prophets, judges, animals) through whom the answer is voiced. This narrative scaffolding — a question routed through a gallery of speaking figures — is what distinguishes the illustrated German Losbuch from a bare lot-table, and makes it one of the most vivid windows onto everyday late-medieval fortune-telling.

## For artists & game designers
A cast of 'oracle NPCs', each delivering a fixed prophetic line — directly usable as a divination interface.

entries.title: Oracle-creature from the Heidelberg Book of Fate (fol. 96v)
entries.work: The Heidelberg Book of Fate (Cod. Pal. germ. 832)
entries.creator: Anonymous (German, workshop of Ludwig Henfflin?)
entries.summary:
A single coloured miniature of an oracle-animal heading its column of text, from the *Heidelberger Schicksalsbuch*.

## Iconography
At the top of the page a framed painting of a beast on a green ground introduces the prognostic text below. Animals recur through the *Losbuch* as pointer-figures, each lending its emblematic character to the fate it announces.

## Significance
The bestiary of the German fate-books — lions, stags, birds, fabulous beasts — turns the casting of lots into a procession through a symbolic menagerie, each animal a node in the divinatory path. The blending of zodiacal, bestiary, and prophetic imagery in a single popular oracle-book exemplifies how late-medieval divination drew freely across the esoteric image-stock.

## For artists & game designers
An emblematic oracle-beast with its prophecy — the kind of symbolic creature-card a fate or tarot system is built from.
